hero.title: Glyph – Open Wearable Platform
hero.oneLiner: An open, modular wearable-gauntlet concept explored through a separate looks-like model and works-like interaction prototype.

context.text: Today's smartwatches make the wrist useful while keeping their sensors and hardware closed. Glyph explores an intentional-wear gauntlet as an open, programmable interface for controlling computers, environments, and agentic systems.

bullets: Built two separate models: a looks-like appearance prototype and a works-like interaction prototype.
The looks-like model uses a transparent thermoformed PETG shell, PETG-GF structure, a curated black acrylic layer, and three diagonal white LED filaments.
The works-like model uses a Waveshare ESP32-S3 Touch LCD 1.83-inch development board and a XIAO nRF52840 Sense Plus secondary module.
Implemented IMU input, BLE-HID paths, dashboard telemetry, Wi-Fi/BLE bridges, and mapped Home Assistant control.
Kept the appearance and functional models visibly separate instead of implying one fully integrated product.

decisions.decision: Split the prototype into looks-like and works-like models
decisions.why: Each artifact could answer one question honestly: what the product should feel like and whether the interaction loop can work.
decisions.tradeoff: The case study must clearly explain that the finished appearance model and working electronics are separate.

decisions.decision: Use transparent PETG over a curated black visual layer
decisions.why: The clear shell communicates the visual direction without pretending the visible composition is the literal working circuit.
decisions.tradeoff: Smoked tint remains a future CMF direction, not the submitted prototype state.

decisions.why: The side attachment supports the gauntlet silhouette and the verified build direction.
decisions.tradeoff: Earlier rear-cartridge studies are historical explorations, not the current product architecture.

decisions.decision: Scope the built light system to three white filaments
decisions.why: It delivered the light language within the prototype window without claiming a fabricated dense matrix.
decisions.tradeoff: The dense white matrix remains production intent only.

worked: The two-model strategy separated form and interaction questions without hiding prototype boundaries.
The current firmware demonstrates a teachable lift, point, adjust, and confirm interaction loop.
The open-platform thesis connects industrial design, embedded systems, and environmental control in one product direction.
didnt: Authentic looks-like and works-like photographs are still missing from the portfolio package.
Haptics, EMG, the dense matrix, a custom PCB, smoked PETG, and the full module ecosystem were not integrated into the built prototype.
Role, collaborators, public links, and the final outcome still need Adi's confirmation before publication.

outcome.text: The evidence currently supports a separate appearance model plus a working interaction stack. The case study remains a draft until authentic build photography, demo proof, credits, and a confirmed outcome are supplied.

reflection.text: The next iteration must integrate the physical and functional models while keeping the boundary between built evidence and production intent explicit.

skills.label: Industrial Design
skills.description: Gauntlet form, transparent-shell CMF, and wearable visual language.

skills.label: Embedded Prototyping
skills.description: ESP32-S3 and nRF52840 interaction paths with IMU, Wi-Fi, and BLE.

skills.label: Interaction Design
skills.description: Gesture calibration, target selection, adjustment, and confirmation loops.

skills.label: Fabrication
skills.description: PETG thermoforming, PETG-GF printing, acrylic layers, and sleeve construction.
